mnemonics.title: ALOHA Vulnerable Time
mnemonics.content: PURE ALOHA = 2T (Two Times trouble), SLOTTED = T (Think half the mess)

mnemonics.title: CSMA Types Order
mnemonics.content: 1-Persistent: ALWAYS transmit when idle; Non-Persistent: NOT always - wait random; p-Persistent: Probability p decides your fate

mnemonics.title: CD vs CA
mnemonics.content: CD = Collision DETECTION (Ethernet - wired - stop and jam); CA = Collision AVOIDANCE (WiFi - wireless - request/ack)

mnemonics.title: Slotted ALOHA Peak
mnemonics.content: Slotted efficiency 36.8% - Remember: 'SLOT-36-8' = SLOT gives almost double of 18%

mnemonics.title: Ethernet CSMA/CD Flow
mnemonics.content: Listen Before Talking - Keep Talking - Stop if Collision - Jam then Wait

acronyms.usage: Layer 2 protocol that controls how devices share the communication medium
Protocol where stations listen to channel before transmitting to avoid collisions

acronyms.expansion: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
acronyms.usage: Ethernet standard - detects collisions and stops transmission immediately, used in wired networks

acronyms.expansion: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance
acronyms.usage: WiFi standard - uses RTS/CTS and acknowledgments to avoid collisions, used in wireless networks
Original random access protocol developed for wireless communication in Hawaii

acronyms.expansion: Request to Send / Clear to Send
acronyms.usage: Handshake mechanism in CSMA/CA where stations request permission before transmitting

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a frame as a person crossing a 2-way street. The vulnerable time is 2T - danger zone extends T before and T after the frame transmission time, like a pedestrian vulnerable to cars coming from both directions
Picture time as a series of boxes (slots) equal to frame size T. You can only jump into a box at the START. If you miss, wait for the next box - no partial entries allowed
Imagine standing at a doorway listening for silence before entering to speak. If you hear voices, wait. If silence, speak up - but others might also be listening and speak too!

Think of multiple people talking simultaneously in a meeting. When they realize they're overlapping, they all shout 'STOP!' simultaneously (jam signal), then each waits a random time before trying again

analogies.analogy: Like a room where anyone can shout anytime. If two people shout at once, neither is heard. Both must wait random times and try again - similar to users transmitting whenever they have data
Like impatient people at a party waiting near the microphone. As soon as someone stops talking, ALL nearby rush to grab the mic - often causing collisions. Low delay but high collision probability
Like a wired office intercom where you can hear others while talking. If you hear someone else, immediately stop (collision detection) and say 'sorry' (jam), then retry after random delay
Like a wireless walkie-talkie where you can't hear while transmitting. You must say 'is anyone there?' (RTS), wait for 'go ahead' (CTS), then transmit - prevents collisions by asking permission first

After a collision, imagine each station picking a random number of seconds from 0-10 to wait. If they pick different numbers, the lower one transmits first. If same number, collision again - pick new random numbers. This desynchronization prevents repeated collisions
